claim: Word-initial glyph constraints (o, c, q, s, d) and word-final glyph constraints (y, n, l, r, o) are not phonological but structural tokens marking word boundaries or grammatical roles (e.g., prefixes and suffixes encoding case or tense), analogous to a morpheme-boundary cipher layered on top of a root encoding.
evidence_for: The top word-initial and word-final glyph sets are small and highly skewed, inconsistent with random sampling from a 25-glyph alphabet
Dominant word 'daiin' (799 occurrences) and 'aiin' (506 occurrences) share the suffix 'aiin', suggesting a recurring morpheme
Positional glyph constraints are a known feature of Voynich scholarship and are statistically robust across the corpus
evidence_against: Natural languages also show strong positional constraints (e.g., English words rarely start with certain consonant clusters), so constraints alone do not distinguish cipher structure from phonology
If initial/final glyphs were pure structural tokens, stripping them should yield a residue with higher entropy and stronger Zipf fit; this has not been confirmed
25 glyphs total leaves limited room for a separate morpheme layer without severe ambiguity
test: Strip the most frequent word-initial glyphs (o, c, q, s, d) and word-final glyphs (y, n, l, r, o) from all tokens to create 'root' forms. Recompute Zipf exponent, hapax ratio, and glyph entropy on the residue corpus and compare to full corpus.
test_metric: Zipf exponent and R-squared of root corpus; hapax ratio of root corpus; glyph entropy of root corpus.
test_threshold: Confirmation: root corpus Zipf exponent increases toward 1.0 (from 0.8946), hapax ratio drops below 55%, and root entropy increases above 3.95 bits. Denial: all three metrics remain within 5% of full-corpus values.

tests_run.test: strip the most frequent word-initial glyphs (o, c, q, s, d) and word-final glyphs (y, n, l, r, o) fr
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If stripping positional boundary glyphs dramatically improves Zipf fit and reduces hapax inflation, it confirms a morpheme-boundary encoding and provides a concrete preprocessing step for decipherment.
